content.full_text:
December 17, 1992

Fair Political Practices Commission  
428 J Street, Suite 800  
Sacramento, California 95814  

Re: Formal Opinion Request  
Mayor Michael Blazenski  

Gentlemen:

Mayor Blazenski has asked me to request of your office a formal opinion respecting potential conflicts of interest he may have under the Fair Political Practices Act.

The facts are:

Mayor Blazenski is the owner of a commercial building and lot located on Main Street in the Town of Mammoth Lakes from which he operates a retail liquor store. The building and lot are encumbered by a purchase money deed of trust securing a promissory note having an approximate outstanding unpaid balance of $40,000.00. The balance should pay off in approximately four (4) years.

Until recently the beneficial interest under the promissory note and deed of trust were held by third parties who are not relevant to this inquiry. Mayor Blazenski has recently been informed that the beneficial interest under the promissory note and deed of trust has been purchased by Dennis Hartman to whom that beneficial interest has been assigned. The promissory note was current when it was assigned and remains current.

Dennis Hartman is the primary stockholder in Mammoth Disposal, Inc. He is also a director and officer of that corporation. Mammoth Disposal, Inc., is the contractor under a long term Refuse Collection Agreement with the Town of Mammoth Lakes dated February 24, 1992. Mayor Blazenski consistently opposed giving this contract to Mammoth Disposal, Inc., without competitive bidding and voted against it.

The political history of the relationship between Dennis Hartman and Mayor Blazenski has been spotty at best. It is fair to say that they have been consistently on the opposite side of issues and that a certain amount of acrimony exists between them.

Fair Political Practices Commission  
December 17, 1992  
Page Two

Mayor Blazenski believes that a major, if not the sole, reason that Dennis Hartman has "bought his mortgage" is to intentionally disqualify him from any future matters involving Dennis Hartman and/or Mammoth Disposal, Inc. Mr. Hartman has stated that he entered into this transaction because it is a good investment for him.

To what extent is Mayor Blazenski so disqualified? What is the effect of a citizen intentionally creating a financial interest in a councilman (through no action of that councilman) for the major or sole purpose of forcing the disqualification of a councilman that the citizen believes is negative to his position? If there is a disqualification, does the one (1) year penumbra for disqualification after payoff of the loan apply?

Sincerely yours,

PETER E. TRACY  
Town Attorney

PET/sc  
xc: Mayor Blazenski  
    Town Manager Glenn Thompson
